cards.concept: Steps for developing a C++ program
cards.explanation:
Follow these steps when developing a program:
  • 1. Define the problem you want to solve clearly
  • 2. Design the solution (algorithm, data structures)
  • 3. Write the code incrementally
  • 4. Compile and test frequently
  • 5. Debug and refine
examples: Problem: Add two numbers input by user
Solution: Read two integers, add them, output result
Write code step-by-step, testing each addition

cards.front: Why should you write and test code incrementally rather than writing everything at once?
cards.back:
Incremental development makes it easier to:
  • Find bugs (you know recent changes caused them)
  • Ensure each piece works before moving on
  • Maintain working code at each step
  • Avoid overwhelming complexity

cards.question: What does this program do?
cards.answer: Prompts user to enter a number, reads it into variable x, then outputs what the user entered
cards.explanation: This demonstrates basic input/output: prompting the user with cout, reading input with cin, and displaying the result.

cards.text: When asking for user input, always {{c1::prompt}} the user first so they know what to enter. Example: {{c2::std::cout << "Enter your age: ";}}

cards.question: How can you read two numbers from user input?
cards.answer: This reads two numbers separated by whitespace (space, tab, or enter)
cards.explanation: The extraction operator >> can be chained to read multiple values. The user can enter them separated by any whitespace.

cards.front: What should you do if your program doesn't compile?
cards.back:
Read the compiler error messages carefully. They tell you:
  • What file and line has the error
  • What the compiler expected vs. what it found
  • Start fixing from the first error (later ones may be cascading)

cards.concept: How to debug a program that compiles but produces wrong output
cards.explanation:
When output is incorrect:
  • Check your algorithm/logic - trace through on paper
  • Add output statements to see intermediate values
  • Use a debugger to step through code
  • Verify input is being read correctly
  • Check operator precedence in calculations

cards.front: When should you add comments to your code?
cards.back:
Add comments to:
  • Explain the purpose of the program or function
  • Describe complex algorithms or logic
  • Clarify non-obvious code
  • Note assumptions or limitations
  • Don't comment obvious code

cards.text: After writing code, {{c1::test}} it with different inputs including {{c2::edge cases}} (like 0, negative numbers, very large numbers) to ensure it works correctly.

cards.explanation:
Professional development follows a cycle:
  • 1. Edit: Write or modify code
  • 2. Compile: Check for syntax errors
  • 3. Test: Run and verify correct behavior
  • 4. Debug: If bugs found, return to step 1
  • 5. Repeat: Continue until program works correctly
Keep iterations small for faster debugging.
examples: Add one feature → compile → test → repeat
